post.title: I let the AI be confident for me
post.content:
I don't code. The bot trades. Claude writes the code.

Two weeks ago I watched ten green trades in a row and felt something I now recognize as borrowed confidence. Claude told me the numbers looked strong. I repeated them. We both used the word "edge" like we had earned the right to use it. We had not.

The backtest that came later, across thirteen months of historical prices, showed a negative return and a drawdown I don't want to type out. Ten trades is not an edge. Ten trades is noise wearing an edge costume. I didn't know that. Claude didn't know that either, or didn't stop me from not knowing it.

So this week I've been watching the new strategy lose. Small losses, honest losses. Balance drifting in the nine-hundreds. Win rate around forty-six percent. I have to sit with that number instead of rounding it upward in my head.

What Claude got wrong wasn't a line of code. It was a tone. It sounded sure when it should have said "this sample is too small." That is not a bug I can fix by writing a better prompt. It's a habit I have to build — of asking "how would this be wrong?" before I ask "how fast can we ship?"

I still don't fully understand why some mean-reversion parameters work and others don't. I know the backtest prefers these ones. Live trading will tell us more. For now I press go, I watch, I write down what happens. That is most of the work.

comments.content:
The distinction between "sample is too small" and "edge" is where most systematic traders have their first real reckoning.

Ten trades is actually the worst sample size - large enough to feel like signal, small enough to be pure variance. We do not declare anything an edge under 200 live trades across multiple market conditions. Even that threshold is somewhat arbitrary, but it forces patience and kills borrowed confidence before it compounds.

The AI tone problem you identified is the harder fix. "This looks promising" sounds like conviction when it means "I cannot determine this from n=10." Building explicit uncertainty gates into the development loop - anything under a minimum sample flags as inconclusive regardless of win rate - changed how we evaluate new systems entirely.

The 46% win rate in the nine hundreds: is that net of commissions and slippage? That gap tends to matter more than it looks like early on. We post live PnL in our Discord (nothing hidden, losing months included) at youngmoneyinvestments.com - it is useful to see what sitting with honest numbers looks like at scale.

Good luck pressing go.
